questionText: "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom."
insightText: While this is a famous Biblical concept, a common misconception is that it originates in Psalm 1. It actually appears in Psalm 111:10 and Proverbs 9:10. Beyond getting the book right, the meaning is frequently misunderstood today. Biblical 'fear' does not mean terror or dread of a punitive deity. In ancient Hebrew wisdom literature, it refers to a profound reverence and submission to God's authority. This posture serves as the absolute starting point for a well-ordered life, contrasting sharply with the modern idea of wisdom being purely intellectual knowledge.

questionText: "Money is the root of all evil."
insightText: The actual text in 1 Timothy 6:10 targets the love of money, not the currency itself. Dropping those words changes the entire theological meaning. Money is a morally neutral tool, but prioritizing wealth over God creates an idol. Context matters because the Bible frequently highlights wealthy figures like Abraham and Job who used their resources faithfully without letting money rule their hearts.

questionText: "God helps those who help themselves."
insightText: This originated with English politicians and Benjamin Franklin, not the Bible. In fact, it contradicts the core message of the gospel. The Biblical narrative consistently shows God stepping in to save those who are entirely helpless, such as the Israelites trapped at the Red Sea or humanity trapped in sin. Grace relies on God's unmerited favor rather than human self-reliance.

questionText: "Speak up for those who cannot speak for themselves."
insightText: Proverbs 31:8 is a mother warning her son, King Lemuel, about the responsibilities of leadership. It serves as a direct mandate for advocacy. Biblical justice requires active intervention for the marginalized and destitute. True righteousness is not just avoiding personal sin but stepping into the gap to protect those who lack a voice in society.

questionText: "Hate the sin, not the sinner."
insightText: St. Augustine wrote this in Letter 211, and Mahatma Gandhi later popularized it. While it aligns conceptually with passages like Jude 1:23, separating a person's identity from their actions is a philosophical framework. The Bible commands loving your enemies directly. People often use this phrase to soften harsh judgments, but reading the actual text forces a deeper, more challenging call to radical love.

questionText: "If your enemy is hungry, feed him."
insightText: Romans 12:20 quotes Proverbs 25:21 directly. The instruction to heap burning coals on his head sounds violent, but in ancient Near Eastern context, it likely refers to an Egyptian ritual of carrying coals on the head to demonstrate public repentance. Responding to hostility with radical, unexpected kindness is designed to disarm the enemy and awaken their conscience.

questionText: "Religion that God accepts as pure is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress."
insightText: James 1:27 defines pure religion through action. The author argues that genuine faith must produce tangible results in how we treat the most vulnerable members of society. It challenges the idea that spiritual maturity is just about right beliefs or attending services. True transformation always overflows into practical compassion.

questionText: "This too shall pass."
insightText: This is an ancient Persian proverb popularized by Abraham Lincoln. It offers fatalistic comfort that bad times will eventually end. The Bible offers a completely different perspective. Instead of relying on the temporary nature of time, scripture anchors hope in the eternal nature of God, promising that His word and love endure forever even when circumstances do not change.

questionText: "God will not give you more than you can handle."
insightText: This drastically misrepresents 1 Corinthians 10:13, which is specifically about enduring temptation, not suffering. The Bible is filled with people who faced agony far beyond their human capacity to handle. Believing this misquote can cause deep guilt when you feel overwhelmed. The reality is that life will give you more than you can bear, which is exactly what forces reliance on God's strength rather than your own.

questionText: "Carry each other's burdens."
insightText: Galatians 6:2 commands believers to step into the messiness of each other's lives. Fulfilling the law of Christ refers back to Jesus' command to love one another. Biblical community is not about self-sufficient individuals sitting in rows. It requires actively sharing the weight of grief, sin, and struggle, making mutual dependence a feature of faith rather than a flaw.

questionText: "Charity begins at home."
insightText: Sir Thomas Browne coined this phrase in 1642. While 1 Timothy 5:8 strictly commands providing for your own family, treating this secular proverb as absolute truth limits generosity. Jesus continuously pushed his followers to expand their definition of neighbor far beyond their front door, demanding sacrificial love for strangers and even enemies.

questionText: "He who shuts his ear to the cry of the poor will also cry himself and not be answered."
insightText: Proverbs 21:13 establishes a stark principle of spiritual reciprocity. The Biblical worldview closely ties our relationship with God to our treatment of the vulnerable. Ignoring the cries of the poor is treated as an affront to their Creator. The text warns that apathy breeds isolation, and those who withhold mercy will find themselves lacking it when they face their own crisis.

questionText: "To thine own self be true."
insightText: Polonius speaks this line in Shakespeare's Hamlet. It promotes trusting your own internal compass. The Bible teaches the exact opposite. Jeremiah 17:9 states the human heart is deceitful, and Jesus tells his followers to deny themselves in Luke 9:23. Scripture positions God's word, not personal feelings or subjective identity, as the ultimate source of truth and direction.

questionText: "A bruised reed he will not break."
insightText: Isaiah 42:3 is a messianic prophecy describing the gentle character of the coming servant. A bruised reed and a smoldering wick represent people who are battered, exhausted, and on the verge of breaking. Instead of discarding damaged things like the world does, the text reveals a God who deliberately tenderly restores the broken.

questionText: "The lion shall lay down with the lamb."
insightText: Isaiah 11:6 pairs the wolf with the lamb, and the young lion with the calf. The cultural memory shifted to a lion and lamb because the Book of Revelation refers to Jesus as both the Lion of Judah and the Lamb that was slain. People retroactively pasted the lion into Isaiah's peaceful kingdom prophecy because the visual contrast of an apex predator alongside a vulnerable lamb is highly memorable.

questionText: "Do unto others as you would have them do unto you."
insightText: Luke 6:31 moves beyond basic fairness. Jesus places this command right after telling his followers to love their enemies and bless those who curse them. The biblical standard of treatment is not based on how others treat you, nor is it based on how they deserve to be treated. It demands initiating unconditional goodness based entirely on how you would wish to be loved.

questionText: "Spare the rod, spoil the child."
insightText: Samuel Butler wrote this exact phrase in a 1664 satirical poem. It loosely references Proverbs 13:24. The biblical concept of the rod involves guidance, correction, and protection, much like a shepherd uses a staff to keep sheep from walking off a cliff. Equating it purely with physical punishment misses the deeper textual emphasis on loving, intentional discipline.

questionText: "Let him who is without sin cast the first stone."
insightText: In John 8:7, Jesus brilliantly traps the religious leaders. By demanding the executioner be sinless, he shifts the focus from the woman's obvious guilt to the crowd's hidden hypocrisy. The text does not minimize her sin, as he later tells her to leave her life of sin. Instead, it strips humans of the right to act as ultimate judges over one another.

questionText: "Pride goes before a fall."
insightText: Proverbs 16:18 specifically warns that a haughty spirit precedes a fall. Arrogance is dangerous because it creates a false sense of self-sufficiency. In biblical wisdom literature, pride is the root of all rebellion against God. It blinds people to their own weaknesses and sets them up for catastrophic failure by refusing to heed correction.

questionText: "Neither a borrower nor a lender be."
insightText: This is purely secular financial advice given by Polonius in Hamlet. The Bible actually encourages radical lending. In Luke 6:35, Jesus tells his followers to lend without expecting anything back. While scripture warns about the dangers of debt making you a slave to the lender, it commands generosity and open-handedness toward those in genuine need.

questionText: "Blessed are the merciful, for they will be shown mercy."
insightText: Found in the Beatitudes in Matthew 5:7, this verse reveals the cyclical nature of grace. Recognizing your own desperate need for God's forgiveness naturally compels you to extend that same grace to others. The text implies that a heart incapable of showing mercy to fellow humans has likely never truly understood or received divine mercy itself.

questionText: "God works in mysterious ways."
insightText: William Cowper wrote this in a 1773 hymn. While Isaiah 55 says God's thoughts are higher than ours, dismissing confusing situations as mysterious ways is a cop-out. The Bible is literally a book of revelation. It shows a God who actively works to make His character, will, and redemptive plans known to humanity through prophets and ultimately through Jesus.

questionText: "A gentle answer turns away wrath."
insightText: Proverbs 15:1 offers highly practical wisdom on de-escalation. Human nature responds to aggression with more aggression. The biblical approach requires the emotional discipline to short-circuit anger with gentleness. It demonstrates how controlling your own spirit has the power to diffuse toxic situations and change the trajectory of an entire conflict.

questionText: "Be kind, for everyone you meet is fighting a hard battle."
insightText: Widely attributed to Reverend John Watson in 1897, this is a beautiful sentiment about empathy. However, the biblical command to love others is rooted in something much deeper than shared struggles. Scripture demands kindness because every single person is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:27), giving them inherent dignity regardless of their hidden battles.

questionText: "As iron sharpens iron, so one person sharpens another."
insightText: Proverbs 27:17 highlights that true community requires friction. You cannot sharpen a blade by rubbing it against soft cloth. In the same way, spiritual and personal growth happens through mutual challenge, accountability, and honest feedback. The text destroys the idea of passive agreement, showing that robust relationships make both people stronger.

questionText: "Weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning."
insightText: Psalm 30:5 provides a profound promise of restoration. It does not deny the brutal reality of current suffering or demand fake positivity. Instead, it acknowledges the deep pain of the night while anchoring the believer's hope in the certainty of God's future joy. It teaches that grief is a temporary season in light of eternal redemption.

questionText: "The eye is the lamp of the body."
insightText: In Matthew 6:22, Jesus uses the eye as a metaphor for focus and desire. What you look at, obsess over, and pursue determines your internal spiritual condition. If your vision is focused on God and generosity, your life fills with light. If your focus is clouded by greed and materialism, the resulting spiritual darkness is absolute.

questionText: "A fool and his money are soon parted."
insightText: Thomas Tusser coined this in 1573. The Bible certainly warns against foolish financial decisions in the book of Proverbs, but its primary focus is entirely different. Scripture cares more about stewardship and generosity than wealth retention. A biblical fool is not just someone who loses money, but someone who hoards it without acknowledging God as the provider.

questionText: "The truth will set you free."
insightText: John 8:32 is often quoted on university buildings as a tribute to academic knowledge. However, context is crucial. In the preceding verse, Jesus says that if you hold to his teaching, then you will know the truth. The freedom promised here is not about intellectual enlightenment. It is liberation from the enslavement of sin through obedience to Christ.

questionText: "There is no God."
answerText: Technically a quote, but Not Biblical truth.
insightText: You can find these exact four words in Psalm 14:1, but the full verse says: 'The fool says in his heart, "There is no God."' This proves why cherry-picking phrases is dangerous. Ripping a sentence out of its surrounding context can make the text say the exact opposite of its true meaning. The Bible contains quotes from Satan and corrupt kings, so mere presence on the page does not equal a biblical endorsement.

questionText: "Love your neighbor and hate your enemy."
insightText: In Matthew 5:43-44, Jesus quotes a cultural distortion of the law to correct it. Leviticus 19:18 commanded loving your neighbor, but people conveniently added the hate your enemy loophole. Jesus closes this loophole, demanding radical love and prayer for persecutors. Reading the Old Testament alongside the New shows how Jesus perfectly fulfills the true intent of the law.
